title: Free Pine indicator for chart reading and pressure shifts
author: BeingAffectionate986

comments.author: dogazine4570
comments.body:
Thanks for sharing this. A couple of questions that might help others evaluate it:

- How are you defining “pressure shifts”? Is it based on volume delta, candle structure, volatility expansion, or a custom calculation?
- Does it repaint at all (especially on lower timeframes)?
- Have you tested it across different market conditions (ranging vs. trending)?

If possible, a few annotated chart examples showing how you’d actually trade it (entries/exits, invalidation points) would make it much more useful. Indicators that highlight structure shifts can be powerful, but context and confluence usually matter more than the signal alone.

Appreciate you making it free—looking forward to seeing more details on how you use it in practice.

replies.author: BeingAffectionate986
replies.body:
Appreciate the thoughtful questions. Pressure Wave uses a custom calculation built around directional pressure, participation intensity, impulse expansion vs. repair, and internal disagreement rather than a traditional buy/sell signal model. It’s meant more as a visual context tool than a standalone execution system.

On repainting: it does not intentionally use future-looking logic. Like many real-time visual tools, the live bar can evolve until the candle closes, but confirmed bars are the reference.

I’ve tested it across both trending and ranging conditions, and one of the main reasons I built the FAST/SLOW structure was to help separate cleaner expansion from repair/chop. That said, I wouldn’t recommend using it in isolation. Context, structure, and confluence still matter more than the indicator alone.

Fair point on examples too — annotated chart examples showing how I actually read it would probably make the post much more useful.

replies.author: BeingAffectionate986
replies.body: Its value increases at confluence. A pressure shift in the right location can help distinguish continuation from rejection.
